t: Evidence of medical condition (autoimmune, infectious, endocrine, metabolic, neurological) or substance effect.
Psychosis does not occur in the context of delirium (but may overlap with delirium).
Not better explained by another primary psychotic disorder.
Graus F. et al. Lancet Neurol 2016 — autoimmune encephalitis clinical approach.
Clinical suspicion — new-onset psychosis, especially with atypical features or in young women.
Complete blood count, liver/renal, electrolytes (Na, Ca), glucose, ammonia;
Lumbar puncture — pleocytosis, antibody panel (anti-NMDA-R, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, paraneoplastic).
Tumor screening (when paraneoplastic antibody positive — ovarian USG, pelvic MRI, chest CT).
Medical cause absent; typical clinical course.
Fluctuation of attention; psychosis in the context of delirium.
Autoimmune encephalitis — IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days → IVIG and/or plasmapheresis; second-line rituximab, cyclophosphamide;
Medication-induced — discontinuation of the suspected agent.
— Risk of NMS and disease exacerbation;
ECT — alternative for refractory catatonic or autoimmune encephalitis.
Graus F. et al. Lancet Neurol 2016 — autoimmune encephalitis protocols.
4. — Until etiological treatment produces results; quetiapine, olanzapine superior.
Effective in catatonic or immunotherapy-resistant autoimmune encephalitis.
Most cases respond to etiological treatment; reversible condition.
Anti-NMDA-R encephalitis — 75% good recovery with early immunotherapy.
3–8% secondary etiologies; atypical features require attention; medical evaluation is mandatory in first-episode psychosis.
Myth 2: "Typical antipsychotic (haloperidol) is safe for every psychotic episode"
If suspicion of autoimmune encephalitis, haloperidol and similar first-generation antipsychotics.
Myth 3: "Anti-NMDA-R encephalitis is rare, not worth looking for"
In a young woman atypical psychosis + movement symptoms + seizures is the classic presentation of Anti-NMDA-R; antibody test saves lives; reversible condition.
Myth 4: "If no medical cause is found, a psychiatric diagnosis is made — no further search is needed"
Atypical clinical presentation requires expanded investigation (CSF, MRI, paraneoplastic panel).
Myth 5: "Immunotherapy is not suitable for psychiatric conditions"
If evidence of autoimmune encephalitis, immunotherapy first-line; standard antipsychotic ineffective.
Graus F. et al. A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis. Lancet Neurol 2016;15(4):391–404.
Dalmau J. et al. Anti-NMDA-receptor encephalitis: case series and analysis of the effects of antibodies. Lancet Neurol 2008;7(12):1091–1098.
Levenson J.L. (ed.). APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine. 3rd ed. 2018.
